speaker: interviewer
text: I guess we're rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: The survivor is Nathan Fefer, F-E-F-E-R. The date is June 30, 1995. This is tape number four.

speaker: interviewer
text: [INAUDIBLE] a second. So when we start, he'll just introduce his wife and daughter and then he'll tell us about the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --OK?

speaker: interviewer
text: Sweet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you introduce us to your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my wife Anna. This is my daughter Fay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, you have another daughter who's not here, right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, one other daughter's in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marilyn.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell us about the tallis that you're wearing.

speaker: interviewee
text: See, when I was preparing to run away, you know, I picked up a tallis in ghetto, because plenty tallisim was in ghetto. And I smuggled this into the-- the camp, you know. Because sometimes, you know, they search you if you went into the camp, but sometimes they didn't bother you a lot. So I went in this, and then later, I took it with me [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had it with you when you went into the camp for the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I-- I got this in camp, you know, when I was there when I was working in the-- in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, when you were working in the ghetto.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the ghettos.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then you kept it with you from then on?

speaker: interviewee
text: From there until I run away. Because, you know, in camp at night, we used to make a minyan there too and daven in camp. In camp at night. Not everybody, because there was a small room, but in camp at night, you made a minyan there and we daven in camp. At night in camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know which night it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, sure they knows. People knows.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you eat that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't. There was nothing to eat there now, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: But did-- the bread or the soup that they gave you--

speaker: interviewee
text: We saved-- we saved for the-- for supper, you know.

speaker: interviewer
text: What else do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: This [INAUDIBLE], you know. Tefillin, you put that [INAUDIBLE]. They were old already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did this come from the ghetto, too?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that your husband's experiences during the war affected the kind of man that he became?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I mean, I-- I only knew him after the war. I didn't know him before. When I met him, it was after the war, in 1945. In Gdansk. My sister was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: You don't want to hear my story now? Or can I tell you partially how I met him? You can tell us how you met him.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister was liberated a year before me in, 1944, because [INAUDIBLE] in Poland. I was the whole year, 1944, and the beginning 1945 in May when the end-- the end-- the war ended, I was still with the Germans. I was liberated May, 1945, the 5th of May. And then I started heading towards home to find out where I was with the Poles-- the volksdeutsche, they were-- to find out if perhaps there-- some-- some family are still alive.

speaker: interviewee
text: It took me six weeks to get there, because you had to do-- go through a camp where they searched. There were a lot of, you know, collaborators with the Germans. And I thought I was so-- I said oh. I heard at night they were lying there saying what they're going to-- what lie they're going to say. I said, I'm telling the truth. I'm a Jew-- to the Russians-- why should I hide? I did it.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- when I got there, of course, he started-- he had to listen to me. He started yelling at me. And I was so naive, I cried. But this is not the point. He didn't do me anything, and after the sixth week, this-- everyone had to go to there. You had to wait for your turn to be examined from the-- from the Russian, whoever it was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I finally got to my hometown, and nobody was there except one family. Not even from [INAUDIBLE]. They were from Russia. I could see my home from far. I could see our store in the middle of the city. And this-- we had to-- but this wasn't the place where-- being two weeks in my-- in my hometown with the two people, a family with a child, I guess. So they-- a letter, they showed me, was made. And the envelopes was-- everything was a problem there. So they made from a sheet of paper. You write on it, then you make it into a triangle for envelope. Then I opened up the letter, and this trouble that my sister sent, she knew that I was alive last year, but she didn't know that I survived. So she sent the letter after the war to my hometown, and this family picked up the letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: How it got it, I don't even remember. I opened up, I fainted. I recognized my older sister's handwriting. And then we start heading back. When the Germans and the Russians finished the war, they made the agreement between then. They divided Poland into two. On one side of there was a river called the Bug, B-U-G, where the Russians. On the other side were the Germans, Poland. They're both part of Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So everybody was heading towards Poland. We didn't want to remain in the Russian territory. Also took some time till we got there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went searching. Here on the train going-- coming to [INAUDIBLE], I found out-- they told me that my sister was here last year. So I already had a sign of life, just one sister. And then I found out from the letter that the other sister's alive in deep Russia.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I started heading. They told me the older the Jews, the survivors, used to concentrate in one place. First, [INAUDIBLE]. The name is familiar to you. I got there. They said they were here, and they went further.

speaker: interviewee
text: The next one was Lublin. I went there, and they said, she was here with a group. And then they told me Lodz. Maybe this is familiar to you, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from Lodz I headed to Gdansk. In Lodz I found out that she is really in Gdansk. And I came Gdansk, and she didn't know that I'm coming. And this is when I met him. OK, I have to finish? OK. So you met together there?

speaker: interviewee
text: We met there. Not with him right away. My sister-- he knew my sister. He came into the house, he was doing a little business here. You have to live, make a dollar. A dollar. It wasn't dollars. And this-- this is when I met him, in Gdansk.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how long after you met did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, this was-- this was still in the-- in the beginning of the '40s.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did-- and-- and when did the two children come into the picture?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah the other one was--

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't wait too long.

speaker: interviewee
text: She was the same year. Yeah, nine months later she was born. But my old-- my younger one, I had to go to work so I couldn't afford it to have another child.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she was born--

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was born six years later.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember when you were growing up, did your parents talk about their experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: All the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: All the time. Did you have other friends whose parents were survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: They weren't personal friends of mine, per se. I didn't go to school  with any, but some of the parent-- some of the children of the parents that my parents were friendly with and they used to get together. Used to call them [INAUDIBLE]. And they used to sit and talk Yiddish and all tell stories, and their children-- we were-- you know, we were friends somewhat, but not in the same community, basically.

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you think that their-- that their experiences impacted the kind of people that they are?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I think there's even no way to describe how that changed their lives. I think if you-- I had to use one word, I would say that it made them very angry people. And I don't think that they would have probably been like that had it not been for the experiences. And I think it's pretty well justified.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what kind of impact has it had for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister always used to say that the children of survivors have a lot of guilt. And I think that's pretty accurate.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is there a message that you would like to give each other on this tape?

speaker: interviewee
text: To live another three years.

speaker: interviewee
text: They just want to see great-grandchildren. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Great-grandchild. We lived to see our granddaughter to get married. And this was only-- this was our prayer, just to see Leah's-- after Leah's wedding, he said, I don't care what's happen. But now that, thank God, she's married, we want a little bit more. But she tells us to wait another two years. She's in school and working.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I told her, I don't have time to wait. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: In closing, is there any last words that you would like to say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Should we have--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'd like to know why do the children feel guilty?

speaker: interviewer
text: Can-- can you talk to us about why you feel guilty?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think because they feel guilty because they survived and-- and many members of their family didn't. So I think to some extent you have a great deal of guilt about that. And so then you almost feel the same way because, you know, like, why are you here? You wouldn't be here if they weren't here. That-- that kind of syndrome. But then you also have to admire their unbelievable, uncanny, wiles to how they ever managed to get through all of this. And I have a cousin who, at the time, was, like, 13 years old by himself. And it's just like a story that somebody wrote that there's no way that anybody could write a story about some of these things that these people did. It just-- you don't think that a human being would be able to do all these things to make themselves survive. It's just unbelievable.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you have any sense of why you think your father survived?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think he's a very smart person. And--

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think he was strong. And I think he's got an amazing will to live, because, like he just said now, I remember before he always sets a goal for himself. First he wanted to live till my cousin's bar mitzvah. That was the big thing. Then he wanted to live to see his granddaughter. Then he did that. Then he wanted to live to see his granddaughter get married. Now he wants to live-- and he'll probably do it. I guess maybe 'stubborn' is the word. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: By the way,  my nephew wasn't 13. He was only nine.

speaker: interviewee
text: Better yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother's youngest boy. And he went through an ordeal.

speaker: interviewee
text: Everybody had to.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kid. My story, I'll tell you later.

speaker: interviewee
text: We just hope for a-- for a better world.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I-- I just hope that--

speaker: interviewee
text: To live together  with everybody in peace.

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes I-- I look at the Jews, mainly Jews, but I figure they don't do enough for the Jewish people-- excluding you-- and Israel. And this is our aid. You know, they stay aside. We belong to every existing organization, and we are not rich people at all. We give away from ourselves, but this is our voice for reality. The Jewish state and the Jewish people. And I would like to see more people do the same. Not the same, but be involved.

speaker: interviewee
text: If we would have Israel in that time--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this would never happen.

speaker: interviewee
text: --Hitler wouldn't do this. Hitler wouldn't do this. This-- this Israel would give him a lesson, but--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to thank you very much--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, thank you for your effort.

speaker: interviewee
text: You're welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: --for agreeing to share your story.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it's very--

speaker: interviewer
text: It was great.

speaker: interviewee
text: --very nice. Not too many American kids have been--

speaker: interviewee
text: They're not American. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, kids.

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken in Poland after the war. And her name is-- our neighbor, she my life-- [INAUDIBLE], and her brother [INAUDIBLE]. This was in Poland in Belzyce.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my picture, and this is my wife picture. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sitting there, and your [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife picture. This picture was taken in Germany in DP camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Point to your picture again, and look at the monitor when you do it so we can see.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is my. And this is my wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's your wife? That's great. OK, thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me--

speaker: interviewer
text: No. When he says 'speed.' Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: --and this is my wife. This picture was taken in DP camp in '46.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is you and your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is me and my wife. Nathan and Anna.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. Speed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell us what this is?

speaker: interviewee
text: This picture was taken from the-- from Israel. And they took a picture from the people what I was hiding there. This is Wladyslawa and Marianne in Belzyce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they get $35--

speaker: interviewer
text: Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is Nathan Fefer, and this is my wife Anna. And this is my daughter Fay. And this is my daughter Marilyn. This is my granddaughter Leah.

speaker: interviewee
text: [INAUDIBLE]

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know when this picture was taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was when she was about 12, 13 years old, my granddaughter.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember when we took the picture. I think the year was in--

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut please. [INAUDIBLE]. Speed.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is me and my wife Anna. And this picture was taken on my wife's birthday in '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: March of--

speaker: interviewee
text: March, '95.

speaker: interviewer
text: Cut.
